name: Islamic Theology
title: Allah - Islamic Theology
description: Also known as The 99 Beautiful Names (Asma al-Husna). Associated with All Creation, All Knowledge, All Power. Known by the epithets: Ar-Rahman (The Compassionate).

cross_cultural_parallels.name: God of Wisdom & Writing | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Dhikr (Remembrance of God)
worshipPractices.description: Dhikr involves the repetitive utterance of God's names, often using prayer beads (tasbih). This practice aims to purify the heart and strengthen the connection between the individual and Allah. It is a form of devotion and a way to seek closeness to the Divine.

worshipPractices.name: Du'a (Supplication)
worshipPractices.description: Du'a is personal prayer and supplication to Allah, asking for guidance, blessings, and forgiveness. It is a direct communication with the Divine and a way to express one's needs and desires. Muslims believe that Allah answers all prayers, though not always in the way we expect.

festivals.name: Eid al-Fitr
festivals.description: Eid al-Fitr celebrates the completion of Ramadan, the month of fasting. It is a time of feasting, prayer, and giving to charity (Zakat al-Fitr). Muslims gather for special prayers and celebrate with family and friends, exchanging gifts and sharing meals.

festivals.name: Eid al-Adha
festivals.timing: Celebrated on the 10th day of Dhu al-Hijjah, the 12th month of the Islamic calendar
festivals.description: Eid al-Adha commemorates the prophet Ibrahim's willingness to sacrifice his son in obedience to God. It involves the sacrifice of an animal (typically a sheep, goat, or cow) and the distribution of the meat to the poor and needy. Muslims also perform the Hajj pilgrimage to Mecca during this time.

cultural.modernLegacy: The concept of the '99 Names of Allah' continues to inspire artists, poets, and calligraphers, who explore the diverse attributes of God through their work. The emphasis on compassion, justice, and mercy derived from these names promotes ethical behavior and social responsibility within Muslim communities and informs interfaith dialogue.

associations.significance: Represents the 99 Names of Allah, each embodying an aspect of divine theology.

associations.name: Balance
associations.significance: Islamic theology emphasizes balance (Adl) in all aspects of life, reflecting divine justice.

associations.name: Light
associations.significance: Represents divine guidance and the unveiling of truth, a central theme in Islamic theology.

associations.name: Submission
associations.significance: The core of Islamic theology is submission (Islam) to the divine will and commands.

associations.name: Green
associations.significance: Symbolizes Paradise and spiritual growth, reflecting the ultimate goal in Islamic theology.

associations.name: Knowledge
associations.significance: Islamic theology emphasizes the pursuit of knowledge as a path to understanding God.

associations.name: Compassion
associations.significance: Reflects the divine attribute of Ar-Rahman, central to understanding God's relationship with humanity.

companions.name: Mika'il
companions.description: An archangel of mercy and rain, often depicted as a helper and protector of the righteous, acting under the command of Allah. He is responsible for distributing sustenance to all creation.

companions.name: Jibril
companions.description: The archangel who delivers divine messages and revelations from Allah to prophets, most notably to Muhammad. He serves as a vital link between the divine and the human realm.

companions.name: Israfil
companions.description: The archangel who will blow the trumpet to signal the Day of Judgment. He stands ready to fulfill Allah's command.

companions.name: Azrael
companions.description: The angel of death, responsible for taking souls at the end of their earthly lives. He performs this duty as decreed by Allah.

extendedContent.title: The Creation Narrative: From Nothingness to Existence
extendedContent.content:
Islamic theology posits a universe created ex nihilo by Allah (God). The Quran (Surah Al-Anbiya, 21:30) states, "Have those who disbelieved not considered that the heavens and the earth were a joined entity, and We separated them and made from water every living thing? Then will they not believe?" This verse alludes to a primordial unity shattered by divine will, establishing a foundational concept within Islamic cosmology. The creation process is described as a swift act of divine command, 'Kun!' (Be!), and it is (fayakun) – it is (Quran, Surah Ya-Sin, 36:82). This demonstrates Allah's absolute power and authority over existence. The initial creation includes angels, including Jibril (Gabriel), who serves as the messenger between Allah and the prophets. 

The creation of Adam, the first human, is a pivotal moment. Fashioned from clay (Quran, Surah Al-Hijr, 15:26), Adam is granted knowledge and dominion over the earth. The story of Adam and Eve in Paradise, their temptation by Iblis (Satan), and their subsequent expulsion is central to understanding human nature, free will, and the consequences of disobedience. Iblis' refusal to bow before Adam, citing his own creation from fire as superior to Adam's creation from clay, establishes the concept of arrogance and rebellion against divine command, which form the basis for the concept of evil.

The specific details of the creation narrative, including the length of the creation days (Quran, Surah Fussilat, 41:9-12), have been subject to various interpretations within Islamic scholarship. Some interpret these days literally, while others understand them metaphorically as periods of vast time. Regardless of the interpretation, the creation narrative serves as a testament to Allah's omnipotence, wisdom, and the purpose behind creation, emphasizing the importance of humanity's role as stewards of the Earth.

extendedContent.title: The Significance of Prayer (Salah) and Divine Presence
extendedContent.content:
Prayer (Salah) in Islam is not merely a ritualistic act but a direct connection between the believer and Allah. The Quran emphasizes the importance of establishing prayer (Quran, Surah Al-Baqarah, 2:3), describing it as a means of remembrance (Dhikr) and a shield against wrongdoing (Quran, Surah Al-Ankabut, 29:45). The five daily prayers (Fajr, Dhuhr, Asr, Maghrib, and Isha) serve as constant reminders of Allah's presence throughout the day, anchoring the believer in a state of mindfulness and devotion.

The direction of prayer (Qibla) towards the Kaaba in Mecca symbolizes the unity of the Muslim community and their collective devotion to Allah. The Kaaba, believed to have been built by Abraham and his son Ishmael (Quran, Surah Al-Baqarah, 2:127), is not an object of worship itself but a focal point for prayer and a symbol of monotheism. The physical postures of prayer – standing, bowing, prostrating – are expressions of humility and submission to Allah's will. The recitation of Quranic verses during prayer serves as a form of communication and reflection on divine guidance. 

Beyond the obligatory prayers, Muslims are encouraged to engage in voluntary prayers (Nawafil) and supplications (Du'a) to further strengthen their connection with Allah. These acts of devotion allow for personal expression of gratitude, seeking forgiveness, and requesting guidance in all aspects of life. The concept of 'ihsan' (excellence) in worship emphasizes the importance of performing prayers with sincerity, mindfulness, and a deep awareness of Allah's presence. This striving for excellence in worship is believed to bring the believer closer to Allah and enhance their spiritual well-being.

extendedContent.title: The Role of Angels (Mala'ika) in Divine Governance
extendedContent.content:
Angels (Mala'ika) are integral to the Islamic understanding of the cosmos, acting as intermediaries between Allah and the created world. They are created from light (Nur) and are incapable of disobedience, constantly glorifying Allah (Quran, Surah Al-Anbiya, 21:20). The most well-known angel is Jibril (Gabriel), the messenger who conveyed divine revelations to the prophets, including the Quran to Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). Jibril's role underscores the importance of divine communication and guidance in Islamic theology.

Other prominent angels include Mika'il (Michael), often associated with rain and sustenance, and Israfil, who will blow the trumpet on the Day of Judgment (Quran, Surah Az-Zumar, 39:68). Azrael, the angel of death, is tasked with taking souls at the time of passing. Munkar and Nakir are two angels who question individuals in their graves about their faith. The existence and specific roles of these angels are detailed in the Quran and Hadith (sayings and actions of Prophet Muhammad).

Angels are also believed to record the deeds of individuals, both good and bad, which will be presented on the Day of Judgment (Quran, Surah Al-Infitar, 82:10-12). This belief reinforces the concept of accountability and the importance of righteous actions. The presence of angels is a constant reminder of Allah's watchfulness and the divine order that governs the universe. Their existence and roles are not merely symbolic but represent a complex and multifaceted system of divine governance.

extendedContent.title: The Day of Judgment (Yawm al-Qiyāmah) and Divine Justice
extendedContent.content:
The Day of Judgment (Yawm al-Qiyāmah) is a central tenet of Islamic belief, representing the culmination of human existence and the ultimate realization of divine justice. The Quran describes it as a day of immense upheaval and cosmic transformation, when the earth will be leveled, the mountains will crumble, and the oceans will boil (Quran, Surah At-Takwir, 81:1-6). This cataclysmic event signals the end of the world as we know it and the beginning of a new reality. The trumpet will be blown by the angel Israfil, signaling the resurrection of all individuals from the beginning of time.

On this day, each person will be judged based on their deeds, both good and bad, as recorded by the angels. The Quran describes a scale (Mizan) upon which these deeds will be weighed (Quran, Surah Al-A'raf, 7:8-9). Those whose good deeds outweigh their bad deeds will be admitted to Paradise (Jannah), a realm of eternal bliss and reward. Those whose bad deeds outweigh their good deeds will be consigned to Hellfire (Jahannam), a place of torment and punishment.

The concept of intercession (Shafa'a) on the Day of Judgment is also significant. Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) is believed to have the ability to intercede on behalf of his followers, seeking Allah's mercy for those who have committed sins. However, intercession is ultimately subject to Allah's will and cannot be guaranteed. The Day of Judgment serves as a powerful reminder of accountability, encouraging believers to strive for righteousness and to seek Allah's forgiveness for their shortcomings. It underscores the importance of living a life guided by Islamic principles and the consequences of neglecting divine commandments.

shortDescription: In Islamic theology, Allah is the supreme and all-encompassing deity, the sole creator, sustainer, and judge of the universe, revered for absolute power and knowledge.

sources.author: Annemarie Schimmel
sources.title: Mystical Dimensions of Islam
sources.description: Explores the profound mystical aspects of Islam, providing insights into the understanding of Allah in Sufi traditions.

sources.author: Toshihiko Izutsu
sources.title: God and Man in the Koran
sources.description: Analyzes the theological concepts within the Quran, focusing on the relationship between Allah and humanity.

sources.author: W. Montgomery Watt
sources.title: Muhammad: Prophet and Statesman
sources.description: Offers historical context and theological implications of Muhammad's teachings concerning Allah.

subtitle: The Supreme Being, Creator and Sustainer of All Existence
